one_line_takeaway: ARuleCon combines a vendor-neutral rule IR, an iterative agentic-RAG documentation-retrieval loop, and an executable Python-based cross-rule consistency check to convert SIEM detection rules across five vendor query languages, beating direct-LLM-prompting baselines by roughly 8-13% on average across CodeBLEU, embedding similarity, and logic-slot-consistency metrics (matching the abstract's ~15% headline figure at the high end) at roughly 10x the token cost and latency.
key_finding: Table 3 (per-model, per-direction results) shows ARuleCon (AC) beats the baseline (BL) on all three similarity metrics for every one of the 20 source->target vendor pairs and all three backbone models, e.g. Splunk->Microsoft Sentinel with GPT-5: CodeBLEU 63.7 vs 58.9, Embedding Similarity 69.3 vs 63.8, Logic Slot Consistency 73.2 vs 67.6. Aggregated gains: for GPT-5, +9.1% CodeBLEU, +10.3% embedding similarity, +11.6% logic slot consistency over baseline; DeepSeek-V3 +8.4%/+11.1%/+13.0%; LLaMA-3 +9.7%/+10.8%/+12.1% -- matching the abstract's claimed ~15% average outperformance across the full range (paper states relative gains vary 5-15% depending on direction/metric). Execution-success rates (Table 4) for ARuleCon-only conversions are mostly >=90%, with Google Chronicle and Splunk targets near 100%, while IBM QRadar and RSA NetWitness targets fall to 92-97% (lowest observed: Splunk->IBM QRadar at 0.774). Efficiency (Table 5): ARuleCon costs far more than baseline -- GPT-5: 20,184 prompt / 3,042 output tokens, $0.046, 142s generation time vs baseline's 2,137/514 tokens, $0.008, 12s; similar ~10x token and ~10x time overhead for DeepSeek-V3 (18,532/2,874 tokens, 177s vs 1,954/472 tokens, 16s) and LLaMA-3 (16,947/2,601 tokens, 163s vs 1,806/438 tokens, 18s).
core_contribution: ARuleCon is an agentic LLM framework for converting SIEM detection rules between five heterogeneous vendor rule languages (Splunk SPL, Microsoft Sentinel KQL, IBM QRadar AQL, Google Chronicle YARA-L, RSA NetWitness ESA). Its own contribution (not cited prior work) is three pieces used together: (1) a vendor-agnostic conversion Intermediate Representation (IR) defined as IR = <M,S> where M is fixed metadata (rule_name, description, data_source, event_type) and S is an ordered sequence of steps s_i = <KEYWORD, PARAM, DESCRIPTION> drawn from a fixed 15-keyword vocabulary (FILTER, EXTRACT, AGGREGATE, OUTPUT, TRANSFORM, RENAME, LOOKUP, BUCKET, JOIN, FILL, APPEND, SORT, DEDUP, APPLY, DEBUG); (2) an Agentic RAG reflection loop that decomposes draft-rule refinement into a to-do list of subtasks, each iteratively generating/refining retrieval queries against vendor documentation until an acceptance predicate is satisfied, then patching the draft rule; and (3) a Python-based consistency check that compiles both the source IR and the derived target-rule IR into executable Python pipelines, runs them over synthetically generated benign/attack test logs, and diffs the outputs to surface semantic drift (e.g., a dropped GROUP BY) and generate optimization suggestions (Algorithm 1 in the paper).
threat_model: This is not an attacker/defender adversarial paper. The 'threat' addressed is operational/organizational: SIEM platform migrations, mergers/acquisitions, or multi-vendor deployments force manual, error-prone re-authoring of detection rules across syntactically and semantically incompatible query languages; a faulty conversion silently degrades detection coverage (missed alerts) rather than being exploited by an adversary. The paper explicitly frames the stakes as high because 'misconverted rules translate into missed detections, which can propagate into downstream incident-response failures' -- i.e., correctness of the conversion tool itself is the security property being protected, not resistance to a malicious input.
limitations: The paper's own text identifies: (1) failure cases concentrated in IBM QRadar's stateful-vs-stateless rule distinction (stateful multi-event correlation collapses into stateless filters when converted), and temporal/aggregation mismatches when converting from KQL/YARA-L's flexible long-range time binning into platforms with stricter windowing; (2) IBM QRadar and RSA NetWitness show weaker performance attributed to more complex grammar, limited public datasets, and sparser documentation; (3) roughly 10x higher token cost and ~10x longer generation latency versus the direct-prompt baseline, justified by the paper only qualitatively ('15%-accuracy-gain justifies an additional 100-seconds'); (4) the similarity metrics (CodeBLEU, embedding similarity, logic-slot consistency) are proxies for functional equivalence, validated only on a small manually-checked sample (paper states 8/10, 7/10, 10/10 correlation with functional equivalence on 'sampled functionally-equivalent conversions' in an appendix, not the full 1,492-pair set); (5) baseline comparison against code-execution agents like OpenHands/SWE-Agent is mentioned as 'tested' but no table/numbers for this comparison appear in the extracted main-text pages; (6) future work explicitly flags the need for a larger paired source-target corpus to fine-tune/align models for further accuracy gains, implying current results depend on prompting/RAG rather than trained adaptation.

datasets_used: Microsoft Sentinel KQL rules (github.com/Azure/Azure-Sentinel) -- 483 total, 100 sampled
IBM QRadar AQL rules (github.com/Xboarder56/QRCE-Rules) -- 33 total, all used
Google Chronicle YARA-L rules (github.com/chronicle/detection-rules) -- 348 total, 100 sampled
RSA NetWitness ESA rules (github.com/netwitness/nw-esa) -- 40 total, all used
1,492 total source-target conversion pairs constructed by using each of the 5 vendors as source and converting to the other 4
quantitative_results: Splunk->Microsoft Sentinel (GPT-5): CodeBLEU AC 63.7 vs BL 58.9; Embedding Similarity AC 69.3 vs BL 63.8; Logic Slot Consistency AC 73.2 vs BL 67.6
Average gains over baseline: GPT-5 +9.1% CodeBLEU / +10.3% embedding similarity / +11.6% logic slot consistency
Average gains over baseline: DeepSeek-V3 +8.4% / +11.1% / +13.0% on the same three metrics
Average gains over baseline: LLaMA-3 +9.7% / +10.8% / +12.1% on the same three metrics
Relative improvement range across all conversion directions/metrics: +5% to +15%

Execution success rate (Table 4): most target conversions >=90%; Google Chronicle and Splunk targets near 100%; lowest observed Splunk->IBM QRadar = 0.774; IBM QRadar and RSA NetWitness targets generally 92-97% (e.g. Microsoft Sentinel->RSA NetWitness 0.943, IBM QRadar->Microsoft Sentinel 0.970)
Cost/latency (Table 5, GPT-5): ARuleCon 20,184 prompt tokens / 3,042 output tokens / $0.046 / 142s vs Baseline 2,137 / 514 / $0.008 / 12s
Cost/latency (DeepSeek-V3): ARuleCon 18,532 / 2,874 tokens / 177s vs Baseline 1,954 / 472 tokens / 16s
Cost/latency (LLaMA-3): ARuleCon 16,947 / 2,601 tokens / 163s vs Baseline 1,806 / 438 tokens / 18s
baselines_compared: Direct-prompt LLM baseline using the same GPT-5 / DeepSeek-V3 (671B) / LLaMA-3 (405B) backbones and the same prompts as ARuleCon's draft stage, but without the IR, Agentic RAG, or Python-based consistency check

Ablation variants of ARuleCon itself: without IR, without Agentic RAG, without Python-based consistency check (Figure 3, results reported graphically per vendor per metric, not tabulated as exact numbers in extracted text)
Mentioned but not tabulated in the extracted main text: comparison against generic code-execution agents OpenHands and SWE-Agent, stated to be outperformed by ARuleCon
